title: Addiction
subtitle: What the Bible Says About Addiction
summary: Scripture portrays addiction as a form of bondage and offers a path to freedom through dependence on God rather than substances or behaviors that promise relief but deepen captivity.
body:
The ancient world did not use the word addiction, but it understood the reality. Paul describes a pattern every addict recognizes: doing what he does not want to do and failing to do what he knows is right [verse:rom.7.15-20]. This is not a description of casual sin but of a will caught in a cycle it cannot break on its own.

Jesus frames the problem in terms of slavery and freedom. Everyone who sins, he says, is a slave to sin — but if the Son sets you free, you are free indeed [verse:john.8.34-36]. The promise is not that temptation disappears but that a stronger power breaks the cycle. Paul echoes this when he warns that though all things may be permissible, the believer must not be mastered by anything [verse:1cor.6.12].

Recovery in Scripture is never solitary. James urges believers to confess their struggles to one another and pray for healing [verse:jas.5.16]. Galatians pictures the community gently restoring those caught in patterns of sin [verse:gal.6.1-2]. The Bible does not promise instant deliverance in every case, but it insists that no chain is stronger than the God who breaks them.

Freedom often comes through daily dependence — choosing each morning to walk by the Spirit rather than gratify the desires that once controlled [verse:gal.5.16].

verses.annotation: Paul describes the internal war of doing what one hates, a pattern central to the experience of addiction.
Jesus teaches that sin enslaves but the Son's liberation is complete and real.
Paul warns against being mastered by anything, even things that are technically permissible.
Walking by the Spirit as the daily antidote to gratifying destructive desires.
Confession to others and mutual prayer as instruments of healing.
The community's role in gently restoring those caught in repetitive sin.
God breaks the chains of those who cry out from darkness and the shadow of death.

scholars.quote: MacArthur contends that addiction is ultimately an issue of worship — the addict has placed a substance or behavior in the position that only God should occupy, and recovery begins with reordering those loyalties.
scholars.source: The Freedom and Power of Forgiveness (MacArthur, 1998)

scholars.quote: Schreiner reads Romans 7 as depicting the futility of self-reliance against sin's power, arguing that Paul's point is precisely that only the Spirit's intervention breaks the cycle the law cannot.

Fee emphasizes that Paul's warning against being mastered in 1 Corinthians 6 reflects a broader theology of the body as belonging to God, making any form of bodily enslavement a theological issue, not merely a moral one.
scholars.source: The First Epistle to the Corinthians (Fee, 2014)
